texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polymers of intrinsic microporosity (PIMs) are considered as a new class of polymer materials with contorted and rigid backbones [1,2]. The unique  structure  of  PIMs  provides  them  continuously  interconnected networks,  intrinsic  microporosity  of  irregular  shaped  and  superior permeability for CO2. PIM-1 is one of the most widely used PIMs consisting of un-crosslinked polymer chains with solubility in conventional solvents,  these  properties  enable  its  easy  processing.  However,  the comparatively lower selectivity of PIM-1 compared with the commercially available polymer membranes limited its broad application. To decrease the energy input for the processing of gas separation, highly selective and permeable PIM-1 membranes are urgently desired [3 -5].

texts.orig: A B S T R A C T
texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Post-modification  of  the  chemical  structures  can  be  used  to  tailor  the  properties  of  polymers  of  intrinsic microporosity (PIM-1), which shows promise for application of PIM-1 gas separation membrane. Methane sulfonic acid (MSA) is capable of hydrolyzing and crosslinking nitrile groups of PIM-1 to form carboxylic acidcontained  and  triazine  groups  crosslinked  PIM-1  (cPIM-1),  and  simultaneously  catalyze in  situ synthesis  of porous polymer networks (PPNs) in PIM-1 matrix. These reactions were carried out at the same time by a onestep method. Characterization of hydrolysis and crosslinking process of nitrile groups in PIM-1 was performed by ATR, XPS, solubility, and 1 H NMR analysis. MSA catalyzed synthetic approach of PPNs includes the trimerization of three acetyl groups and involves a coupled process of polymerization and membrane architecture formation. Finally, the process of crosslinking and hydrolysis can provide enhanced gas pair selectivity of PIM-1 membrane (cPIM-1) while the gas permeability of the membranes (cPIM-1/PPNs) can be increased by the incorporation of PPNs microstructure networks. As a result, the optimal cPIM-1/PPNs showed Roberson ' s  2008 upper bound separation performance for CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2. The cPIM-1/PPN2-3% membrane demonstrated the best CO2 comprehensive separation performance with the permeability of almost 11511 Barrer, with ideal selectivity of 24.3 and 22.2 for CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4 respectively. Furthermore, the cPIM-1/PPNs membranes show excellent anti-aging properties. This indicates that MSA-catalyzed hydrolysis, crosslinking and in situ synthesis PPNs can effectively adjust the topological structure of PIM-1 membrane for CO2  separation.

Porous additives (zeolites, MOFs, COFs, PAFs, etc.) are added into the PIM-1 matrix to obtain hybrid membranes, which are commonly known as mixed matrix membranes (MMMs) [16 -22]. For post-modification of the chemical structures of PIM-1, crosslinking and hydrolysis both approaches are effective to enhance the selectivity for CO2  because of decreased intermolecular distance. Especially for the process of hydrolysis, nitrile groups in PIM-1 are hydrolyzed into carboxylic acid with the potential to enhance the CO2  diffusion and sorption selectivity. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Another important method has also been reported to enhance the gas pairs  selectivity  and  gas  permeability  of  the  PIM-1 membrane which includes the addition of fillers to obtain hybrid membranes. In 2014, Lau et al. reported that the PIM-1 matrix upon addition of a microporous microparticle -porous  aromatic  framework  (PAF-1)  significantly  enhances the gas permeability and selectivity of the membrane [21]. In addition, the incorporation of PAF-1 inhibited the physical aging phenomena of the PIM-1 polymer chain. After this research, various studies have reported the increase of selectivity and gas permeability of PIM-1 via controlling the filler morphology, size, and concentration as well as tuning the interactions between the PIM-1 matrix and fillers [17 -20, 22]. PIM-1 has been mixed with various fillers including silicate, zeolite, zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs) [29], metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [30,31], graphene [32], graphene oxide [33,34], and inorganic nanoparticles [35]. However, these fillers often show poor compatibility with PIM-1 matrix due to the nature of hydrophobic polymer and hydrophilic fillers. Consequently, the formation of interfacial defects in the resulting MMMs was inevitable, and eventually cause a poor gas separation performance. Kaliaguine et al. suggested that grafting PIM-1 onto the  filler  surface  by in-situ reaction  can  solve  the  poor  compatibility problem, and the membrane prepared by this way has better separation performance  than  that  prepared  by  direct  mixing  [16].  Thus, in-situ synthesis method can effectively solve the problem of poor interfacial compatibility.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Porous polymer networks (PPNs) are a kind of cross-linked microporous polymers that possess a large surface area and rigid backbones. The synthesis of PPNs was carried out by an acid-catalyzed trimerization of three acetyl groups. A diverse library of PPNs has been constructed by using the different crosslinking chemistry and backbone constitutions [36 -40]. In this work, three protocols namely hydrolysis, crosslinking of nitrile groups of PIM-1, and in-situ synthesis of PPNs materials in PIM-1 matrix was accomplished at the same time by the one-step method. The described synthetic strategy of PPNs trimerized the three acetyl groups and entails a coupled process of polymerization and membrane architecture formation. Furthermore, the process of crosslinking and hydrolysis improves the PIM-1 membrane (cPIM-1) selectivity for gas pairs and the incorporation of PPNs microstructure networks are capable to increase the permeability of the membranes (cPIM-1/PPNs) for gases. Finally,  optimal  cPIM-1/PPNs  showed  over  Roberson ' s  2008  upper bound separation performance for CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2. Because of the simple method and promising performance for the gas separation, we supposed that using crosslinking and hydrolysis alongside in situ synthesis  PPNs  to  post  modify  PIM-1  membranes  would  be  an  effective method  to  regulate  its  microstructure  with  improved  gas  separation performance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Monomers including 5,5 ′ ,6,6 ′ -tetrahydroxy-3,3,3 ′ ,3 ′ -tetramethyl1,1 ′ -spirobisindane (TTSBI, 98%, Adamas-beta) and 2,3,5,6-tetrafluoroterephthalonitrile (TFTPN, 98%, Adamas-beta) were used in the synthesis of PIM-1. Before polymerization, TTSBI (13 g) was recrystallized in anhydrous methanol (78 ml) and then vacuum dried at 70 ◦ C. TFTPN was used as received. Superdry 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP) was utilized as a solvent and purchased from MREDA Technology Inc. Potassium carbonate (K2CO3, 99%, J & K)  served  as  a  catalyst  and  used without further purification. Two diacetyl monomers, 1,4-diacetylbenzene  (M1, > 99.0%)  and  4,4 ′ -diacetylbiphenyl  (M2, > 99.0%),  were procured from TCI and Aladdin, respectively. During the catalysis of methane sulfonic acid (CH3SO3H, MSA), M1 and M2 are crosslinked and denoted  as  PPN1  and  PPN2,  respectively.  Tetrahydrofuran  (THF, ≥ 99.9%,  Macklin),  dichloromethane  (DCM, ≥ 99.9%,  MREDA),  and methane sulfonic acid (MSA, 98 + %, Alfa Aesar) were used as received.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2. Synthesis of PIM-1 and PPNs
texts.text: 2.2. Synthesis of PIM-1 and PPNs

texts.label: text
texts.orig: PIM-1 was synthesized via a nucleophilic substitution reaction between TTSBI and TFTPN using K2CO3 as the catalyst in DMF solution. Under a nitrogen atmosphere, TTSBI (120 mmol), TFTPN (120 mmol), and K2CO3 (240 mmol) were poured into anhydrous DMF (500 mL) and heated at 90 ◦ C for 8 h to obtain polymer. Then, the obtained polymer was washed using hot water and ethanol. Finally, they were dissolved into dichloromethane and reprecipitated into methanol. Gel permeation chromatography was used for the determination of the molecular weight of the polymer ( M n = 158699 g mol 1 , M w = 217342 g mol 1 ) of the synthesized PIM-1 using DCM as the fluent. The preparation of PPNs was performed according to the previously reported method [40]. Precisely, M1 or M2 (1 equivalent) and MSA (15 equivalents) were added into a 20 mL glass vial followed by heating in an oven at 110 ◦ C for 12 h. The resulting monolithic solid was washed extensively with water and subjected to refluxed in ethanol for 24 h. The resulting product was vacuum dried at 120 ◦ C for 12 h.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. Fabrication of cPIM-1/PPNs membranes
texts.text: 2.3. Fabrication of cPIM-1/PPNs membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All the membranes were synthesized by following the protocol of the solution-casting method. PIM-1 polymers powder (10 g) were dissolved in  200  mL  THF/DCM  solvents  (5%,  w/v)  and  stirred  for  24  h.  The preparation of the cPIM-1/PPNs composite membranes is demonstrated in Scheme 1a. Precisely, different amount of M1 or M2 (1 wt%, 3 wt%, and 5 wt%) was separately added to the 20 ml of PIM-1 and sonicated for 20 min and stirred for 30 min. The resulting homogenous solution was transferred to a Petri dish and sealed into a self-zip plastic bag. After 2 days, the resulting membranes (PIM-1/M1 and PIM-1/M2) were transferred from the Petri dish to a vacuum oven and heated at 80 ◦ C for 24 h. The fabrication of MSA post-modified membranes includes the following protocol: Initially, both the membranes were soaked in MSA for 10 min, after that they were placed in an oven for crosslinking at 110 ◦ C 48 h. Subsequently, the resulting membranes were washed in deionized water and methanol for several cycles and dried in a vacuum oven at 80 ◦ C for 24 h. The resulted membranes were labeled as cPIM-1/PPNs-X%. For example, cPIM-1/PPN1-1% or PIM-1/PPN2-5%. While pure PIM-1 was used as a control group denoted as cPIM-1. The resulting membranes possess a thickness in the range of 60 -80 μ m.

texts.orig: 2.4. Characterization
texts.text: 2.4. Characterization

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Bruker Avance 400 Spectrometer, 400 MHz was used to record the 1 H NMR spectra of cPIM-1 and PIM-1 by using THFd 8, and CDCl3 as the solvent, respectively. Gel permeation chromatography (GPC, LC-20AD, Shimadzu Co.  Ltd)  was  used  for  the  determination  of  the  molecular weight  of  PIM-1  by  using  DCM  as  the  fluent.  The  attenuated  total reflection mode of Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectra of the polymer films were recorded using a PerkineElmer 782 Fourier transform  spectrophotometer.  To  carry  out  the  wide-angle  X-ray  diffractometry  (WAXD),  we  used  Bruker  D8  Advance  X-ray  diffractometer 3 -40 ◦ ,  5 ◦ min 1 scanning rate, and 1.54 Å of Cu K α radiation wavelength. Bragg ' s equation, d = λ /(2 sin θ ) was used to calculate the d -spacing where λ = 1.54 Å and θ represent the diffraction angle [41]. The N2  adsorption/desorption  isotherms  were  measured  at  77  K  with  a Quantachrome Autosorb-IQ-MP instrument. All of the membranes were degassed  at  100 ◦ C  for  12  h  before  the  sorption  measurements.  The specific surface areas and average pore size diameters were calculated on the basis of the data from the adsorption isotherms with the Brunauer -Emmett -Teller (BET) and HK method, respectively. The thermal performance  of  the  resulted  membranes  was  tested  by  thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, Q5000IR, USA) 30 ◦ C -600 ◦ C temperature range at 10 ◦ C.min 1 under the nitrogen atmosphere. Morphological analysis of the membranes was conducted by using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, TESCAN VEGA 11, Czech Republic).

texts.orig: PIM-1 in CH3SO3H but the nitrile groups of PIM-1 can also be hydrolyzed in the presence of acid and converted into the carboxylic group. Katherine et al. previously reported that PIM-1 can undergo the process of hydrolysis in the mix solution of sulfuric acid and glacial acetic acid, yield  carboxylic  acid-functionalized  PIM-1  (PIM-COOH)  with > 89% conversion in 48 h at 150 ◦ C [14]. Hence, we hypothesized that the PIM-1 could also be hydrolyzed in MSA and partial nitrile groups converted into carboxylic groups. This conversion explains the reason for the partial solubility of cPIM-1 in DMF and (Fig. 2b). While PIM-1 can easily soluble in DMF because of the presence of the carboxylic group but insoluble in DCM [49].

texts.orig: The formation of triazine ring and carboxylic moiety in cPIM-1 was further  characterized  by  the  ATR  spectra,  X-ray  photoelectron  spectrometer (XPS) and 1 H NMR spectra. As shown in Fig. S1, the relative intensity of the characteristic peak of cPIM-1 at 2237 and 808 cm 1 , representing  stretching and  bending  vibrations  of  nitrile  groups, decreased as compared to the pristine PIM-1 membrane [50]. XPS results  of  cPIM-1  are  provided  in  Fig.  3.  The  C  1s  spectrum  of  PIM-1 (Fig.  3a)  can  be  deconvoluted  into  two  peaks  representing  aromatic sp 2 carbon  (at  284.8  eV)  and  nitrile  group  (at  286.7  eV).  The  N  1s spectrum of PIM-1 (Fig. 3b) centered around 399 eV is mainly attributed to the nitrile. After MSA treatment, the C 1s spectrum of cPIM-1 can be deconvoluted into three peaks (Fig. 3c). The newly formed less pronounced peak at 285.5 eV is associated with carboxyl groups [51]. The N 1s spectrum of cPIM-1 can be deconvoluted into two peaks. The newly formed peaks at about 401.5 eV representing the triazine group [13,52]. According  to  the  area  of  N  1s  peak  ratio,  the  crosslinked  degree  of cPIM-1  is  42%.  The  structure  of  the  soluble  parts  of  cPIM-1  was confirmed by 1 H NMR (Fig. 4). The peaks located at 1.72 and 3.58 ppm are attributed to the characteristic peak of THFd 8. A small broad signal at around 10.75 ppm corresponding to the -COOH proton of cPIM-1. The XPS spectra and 1 H NMR spectra clearly demonstrated the formation of the crosslinked structure and carboxylic acid group in cPIM-1.

texts.orig: The microstructures and chain packing of PIM-1, cPIM-1, cPIM-1/ PPNs membranes were studied by XRD analysis (Table 2 and Fig. 8). The characteristic peak of pure PIM-1 was observed at 17.26 ◦ corresponding to the d -spacing of 5.1 Å. For cPIM-1, the characteristic peak (2 θ = 17.56 ◦ )  was  slightly  shifted  towards  smaller d -spacing.  The observed  results  revealed  the  close  packing  pattern  of  the  cPIM-1 polymer chains, because of the crosslinking structure and the hydrogen-bonding interactions between carboxylic acid groups in the cPIM-1  membrane  [54].  For  cPIM-1/PPN1  membranes,  after in-situ incorporation of PPN1, the interstitial distances between cPIM-1 polymer chains will be partially occupied by PPN1, which will reduce the intermolecular  hydrogen-bonding  and  thus  increase  the d -spacing  of cPIM-1. This can explain why cPIM-1/PPN1 has higher d -spacing than cPIM-1. With the increase of PPN1 content, the free volume of cPIM-1 will be occupied by the PPN1. Therefore, the specific surface area and average pore size of cPIM-1/PPN1 membranes decreased with increasing of PPN1 content (Table 2 lists the surface area and average pore  diameter  of  the  membranes  characterized  by  BET  and  HK methods).
texts.text: The microstructures and chain packing of PIM-1, cPIM-1, cPIM-1/ PPNs membranes were studied by XRD analysis (Table 2 and Fig. 8). The characteristic peak of pure PIM-1 was observed at 17.26 ◦ corresponding to the d -spacing of 5.1 Å. For cPIM-1, the characteristic peak (2 θ = 17.56 ◦ )  was  slightly  shifted  towards  smaller d -spacing.  The observed  results  revealed  the  close  packing  pattern  of  the  cPIM-1 polymer chains, because of the crosslinking structure and the hydrogen-bonding interactions between carboxylic acid groups in the cPIM-1  membrane  [54].  For  cPIM-1/PPN1  membranes,  after in-situ incorporation of PPN1, the interstitial distances between cPIM-1 polymer chains will be partially occupied by PPN1, which will reduce the intermolecular  hydrogen-bonding  and  thus  increase  the d -spacing  of cPIM-1. This can explain why cPIM-1/PPN1 has higher d -spacing than cPIM-1. With the increase of PPN1 content, the free volume of cPIM-1 will be occupied by the PPN1. Therefore, the specific surface area and average pore size of cPIM-1/PPN1 membranes decreased with increasing of PPN1 content (Table 2 lists the surface area and average pore  diameter  of  the  membranes  characterized  by  BET  and  HK methods).

texts.orig: For the case of cPIM-1/PPN2 membranes, with the increase of PPN2 content, a new peak was observed at around ~13 ◦ . This new peak is the result  of  the  inefficient  polymer  chains  packing  originated  from  the contortion sites of the PIM-1 backbone. Previous studies [55,56] have reported the XRD data of the dense film of PIM-1 with two characteristic peaks at 13.6 ◦ (6.5 Å) and 18.1 ◦ (4.9 Å) representing the d -spacing of the well packed and loosely packed polymer chains respectively. Initially, the d -spacing  of  the  well-packed  polymer  chains  (2 θ = ~17 ◦ )  was increased  then  start  to  decrease  with  increasing  content  of  PPN2. Furthermore, the intensity of peak at 13.08 ◦ for cPIM-1/PPN2-3% was the highest among three cPIM-1/PPN2 membranes.

texts.orig: In addition, as shown in Fig. 9, the 77 K N2  adsorption/desorption isotherms are performed to characterize the surface area and pore size of PIM-1, cPIM-1, and cPIM-1/PPNs membranes. The BET surface area and average pore diameter of the samples are listed in Table 2. The BET surface area of all cPIM-1/PPNs membranes are lower than that of cPIM1, and with PPNs content increasing, the surface area of the samples gradually decrease and the average pore diameter also decrease except for cPIM-1/PPN2-3%. The variation of pore structure revealed that the PPNs play an important role in tuning polymers ' microstructure. According to the d -spacing values and BET surface area, we can infer that the gas permeabilities of cPIM-1/PPN2 should be higher than that of cPIM-1/PPN1  membranes.  The  SEM  images  of  the  cross-sectional morphology of the cPIM-1/PPNs membranes incorporated with different amounts of PPNs are shown in Fig. S2. All the cPIM-1/PPNs were obtained as dense membranes without any visible defects.

texts.orig: After  treatment  with  MSA,  the  gas  permeability  of  cPIM-1  membranes was significantly reinforced as can be seen in Table 3. This is because of the penetration of MSA molecules into the PIM-1 membranes which  caused  the  protonation  of  the  nitrile  group  and  facilitate  the polymer chain movement. Because of the membrane swelling, the more porous  structures  were  observed  with  enlarged  original  porosities, meanwhile, the process of cross-linking started to occur among polymer chains which solidified the flexible polymer chains. Upon removal of MSA from the cPIM-1 membranes, the recovery of polymer chain from swelled status was quite difficult because the one-dimensional linear polymer chains were transformed into three-dimensional polymer networks. The extensive porosities provide greater gas permeability for the cPIM-1 membranes.

texts.orig: The enhanced gas transportation potential of cPIM-1/PPN2 than that of cPIM-1/PPN1 membranes was associated with the larger pore size of cPIM-1/PPN2 than cPIM-1/PPN1 (Table 2). As mentioned above, cPIM1 and PPNs can form the interpenetrating networks. However, due to the small pore size of PPN1, with the increase of PPN1 content, the wide porosities in cPIM-1 was occupied by PPN1, which in turn reduce the gas transportation across the membranes. As a result, PPN2 increased the inefficiency of chain packing and enhanced the FFV of polymers. This also could be confirmed by d -spacing values and average pore size diameters as discussed above. Whereas, the decrease in gas permeability for the cPIM-1/PPN2-5% may result from the partial agglomeration of PPNs, which can more or less block the interconnected ultramicropores within the cPIM-1 matrix.

texts.orig: The  long-term  aging  behavior  of  the  PIM-1,  cPIM-1  and  cPIM-1/ PPNs membranes with various PPNs loadings were re-measured after 180 days under same measuring condition. The changes in pure gas permeabilities are listed in Table S2. The CO2  permeabilities of all the cPIM-1/PPNs, cPIM-1 as well as the pure PIM-1 are observed to decrease over time. For cPIM-1/PPN2-3%, the CO2 permeability reduced from an average of 11511 Barrer for fresh samples to almost 9936 Barrer for 180day aged samples. This is accompanied by an increase in the CO2/CH4 selectivity from 22.2 to 23.6. According to the long-term aging behavior, the CO2 permeability of cPIM-1/PPNs measured after 180 days is only reduced by about 14%, while that of pure PIM-1 is reduced by 68%. It can  be  deduced  that  the  incorporation  of  PPNs  (form  the  interpenetrating networks with cPIM-1) as well as the crosslinking and hydrolysis  reaction  of  PIM-1  has  positive  effect  on  impeding  the  aging process of polymers with high FFV.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MSA is capable of hydrolyzing and crosslinking the nitrile groups of PIM-1 to form cPIM-1 and catalyze the in-situ synthesis of PPNs materials in the PIM-1 matrix. These reactions were completed at the same time by a  one-step  method.  Confirmation  of  hydrolysis  and  crosslinking  was obtained by ATR, XPS, solubility, and 1 H NMR analysis. In the MSA catalyze synthesis of PPNs, three acetyl groups are trimerized and entail a  coupled process of polymerization and membrane architecture formation. Finally, the crosslinking and hydrolysis reaction could endow PIM-1 membrane (cPIM-1) with improved gas pair selectivity and the incorporation  of  PPNs  microstructure  networks  can  enhance  the  gas permeability  of  the  membranes  (cPIM-1/PPNs).  As  a  result,  optimal cPIM-1/PPNs based CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2 separation performance were over Roberson ' s 2008 upper bound. The cPIM-1/PPN2-3% membrane demonstrated the best CO2 comprehensive separation performance with the permeability of around 11511 Barrer, and ideal selectivity of 22.2 and 24.3 for CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2, respectively. Furthermore, the cPIM1/PPNs membranes show excellent anti-aging properties. This indicates that MSA-catalyzed hydrolysis, crosslinking and in situ synthesis PPNs can effectively adjust the topological structure of PIM-1 membrane for gas separation. In the following study, we will fully discuss the effect of MSA on the hydrolysis and crosslinking of PIM-1 in detail.
